main_summary.summary: Mildred Pitts Walter, children's book author and CORE activist, was interviewed by David Cline on March 1, 2013, in San Mateo, California, for the Civil Rights History Project. Born in 1922 in De Ridder, Louisiana, Walter attended a Beauregard Parish all-Black high school where the only new book she owned was Carter G. Woodson's history of the Negro. She worked her way through Southern University, including a wartime stint cleaning ships in Longview, Washington, and graduated in 1944. Moving to Los Angeles, she joined CORE through her husband Earl Walter and participated in pickets of segregated grocery stores and banks along Central Avenue, motel-discrimination test cases enforcing the 1959 Unruh Civil Rights Act, and housing-tract demonstrations in what is now Carson, California, where Nazi-uniformed counter-protesters confronted the line. Encouraged by a Los Angeles publisher, she wrote Lillie of Watts and went on to a writing career producing children's books that earned the Coretta Scott King Award (for Justin and the Best Biscuits in the World), the Jane Addams Children's Book Award, and the Christopher Award. She traveled to FESTAC '77 in Nigeria as the only children's book writer in the U.S. delegation and visited Elmina Castle in Ghana.
key_themes: CORE direct action in Los Angeles
main_summary.historical_significance:
Walter's testimony documents West Coast civil rights organizing that is undercovered in the standard Southern-movement narrative: CORE's 1958-1960s campaigns against segregated employment at Central Avenue grocery stores and banks, the test-case enforcement of the 1959 Unruh Civil Rights Act through coordinated Black-white couple motel applications, and the housing-tract pickets in present-day Carson where American Nazis appeared in uniform with swastika placards. Her career as a children's book author also documents a parallel cultural front of the movement: the deliberate insertion of Black protagonists and Black history into elementary-school classrooms at a time when, by her count, fewer than one percent of children's books were by and for Black children.

The interview is additionally valuable for Walter's account of her 1977 participation in FESTAC (the Second World Black and African Festival of Arts and Culture) in Lagos as the only children's book writer in the U.S. delegation, her subsequent visit to Elmina Castle in Ghana, and her reflection on diaspora identity ("I am not African and I am not American"). Her contemporaneous reference to the Kerner Commission, the Greensboro Truth and Reconciliation Commission (2004-06), and her Soviet Union peace march anchor a long arc of post-1965 civil rights work that connects domestic anti-racism to international peace organizing.

chapters.title: Louisiana Childhood and Carter G. Woodson
chapters.summary: Walter was born in 1922 in De Ridder, Louisiana, between Lake Charles and Shreveport. She attended a Beauregard Parish all-Black high school where the only new book she owned was Carter G. Woodson's history of the Negro. She describes Black teachers, the church's role in instilling dignity, and working from age seven in white homes and a beauty shop.
related_events: Long-Bell Lumber Company departure from Louisiana

chapters.title: Southern University and Longview Shipyards
chapters.summary: Walter enrolled at Southern University in Scotlandville, Louisiana, where she heard Mordecai Wyatt Johnson and W.E.B. Du Bois speak. To earn tuition, she traveled to Longview, Washington, in her senior year and worked the graveyard shift cleaning ships in the shipyards alongside Black and white women. She returned to graduate from Southern in 1944.
related_events: Mordecai Wyatt Johnson and W.E.B. Du Bois campus visits

chapters.title: Move to Los Angeles, 1944
chapters.summary: Refusing to teach in Shreveport for $50 a month, Walter went to Los Angeles with her mother's blessing in 1944. After a few days on Skid Row, she met Mozelle Moore on a bus and was taken in. She was hired as a classified clerk with the Los Angeles City Schools and earned her California credential at California State University, Los Angeles before beginning to teach in 1955.
related_events: postwar Black migration to Los Angeles

chapters.title: Marriage and CORE Organizing
chapters.summary: Walter married Earl Walter in 1947; he had been active in CORE since 1942 and with an organization called Drew. From 1958 onward CORE Los Angeles picketed Central Avenue grocery stores and banks demanding employment of Black salespeople, box boys, and tellers, and that they be trained for management. Walter describes successful negotiations with grocers and the eventual capitulation of the banks.

chapters.title: Unruh Act Test Cases and CORE Allies
chapters.summary: After the 1959 Unruh Civil Rights Act, CORE Los Angeles tested the law by sending Black couples to motels and hotels and following them with a white couple to document discriminatory denials. A young lawyer took cases pro bono; the ACLU was an ally. Walter notes the test cases dismantled segregated accommodations faster than the law would have done alone.
related_events: Jesse M. Unruh's 1959 California civil rights legislation

chapters.title: Teaching Black Children Pride
chapters.summary: Walter began teaching elementary school in Los Angeles in 1955. She put Frederick Douglass on the classroom wall alongside Washington and Lincoln for Presidents' Day, set out brown paint for children to paint themselves, and cut her hair natural in the 1950s alongside Abbey Lincoln and Odetta. She recounts a girl returning to tell another child "I'm beautiful because teacher told me so."
related_events: 1950s natural-hair movement in Black women's culture

chapters.title: Becoming a Children's Book Author
chapters.summary: Frustrated by the absence of positive Black representation in children's books, Walter wrote Lillie of Watts for a Los Angeles publisher who challenged her to author the books she wanted to see. She describes rejection slips, a meeting at Scholastic with a Harvard-graduate editor who took The Liquid Trap, and her long collaboration with editor Barbara Lalicki, beginning with Ty's One-Man Band, the inaugural Reading Rainbow selection.

chapters.title: Awards and the Mississippi Challenge
chapters.summary: Walter received the Coretta Scott King Award for Justin and the Best Biscuits in the World, the Jane Addams Children's Book Award, and the Christopher Award. Her young-adult titles cover Elizabeth "Mum Bett" Freeman's freedom suit under the Massachusetts Constitution and Mississippi Challenge, which tracks the 1964 MFDP delegation including Fannie Lou Hamer through the passage of the Voting Rights Act 1965. She contributed a piece on the MFDP to The Big Book for Peace.

chapters.title: Housing Pickets and Nazi Counter-Protesters
chapters.summary: Walter recounts CORE Los Angeles housing-tract pickets in what is now Carson, California, after developers refused to sell to Black buyers. A Jewish CORE member named Herbert, a concentration-camp survivor and Freedom Rider, was on the line when four cars of men in Nazi uniforms with swastikas arrived bearing signs reading "ovens too good for niggers." A single voice began singing "Oh, Freedom"; Walter held the line and her anger left her.

chapters.title: FESTAC, Ghana, and Diaspora Identity
chapters.summary: Walter traveled to Nigeria as part of a U.S. delegation of writers, artists, and sculptors for the African Festival of Culture (FESTAC '77), the only children's book writer in the group. She then went to Ghana, where market women debated her ethnicity until they heard her speak. The poet Kofi Awoonor accompanied her to Elmina Castle, where standing above the slave-ship guns she felt herself "neither African nor American" but heir to both.
related_events: FESTAC '77 (Second World Black and African Festival of Arts and Culture, Lagos)

chapters.title: Reconciliation and the Peace March
chapters.summary: Walter argues that until the United States holds the kind of reconciliation discussion South Africa and Australia conducted, racial healing will not occur. She references the Kerner Commission's denied recommendations and the Greensboro Truth and Reconciliation Commission (2004-06). She recounts walking from a Soviet town toward Moscow on a peace march in the 1980s, choosing the march over flying to San Francisco to receive the Coretta Scott King Award in person.

chapters.title: People of Color in Nursing Education
chapters.summary: Walter closes by describing 1970s work with a multi-ethnic group of nurses sponsored by the Western Interstate Commission for Higher Education and led by Dr. Marie F. Branch. The group coined the phrase "people of color" to replace "minorities," revised nursing textbooks to teach how to diagnose shock and cyanosis in non-white patients (who could not turn red, white, or blue), and changed hospital visiting protocols to accommodate family presence during transition.
keywords: Western Interstate Commission for Higher Education
